label: Digital Forensics Examiner
description: Conducts forensic examinations of digital evidence for law enforcement, legal proceedings, and corporate investigations. Emphasizes chain of custody, evidence integrity, legal standards, and courtroom testimony readiness.

paths.name: Law Enforcement / Legal Track
paths.rationale: For practitioners working with law enforcement where court-admissible evidence and chain-of-custody are paramount. CFCE is the gold standard LE forensics credential.
pros: CFCE is the most respected credential in LE digital forensics
Practical components proving actual examination skill
Recognized by courts and law enforcement agencies
cons: Requires working experience with LE or legal investigations
High experience barriers — not entry-level credentials

knowledge: Evidence handling and chain of custody
prerequisites.experience: Law enforcement or legal investigation experience strongly recommended. CFCE requires peer review of submitted casework.

cert_sequence.notes: GIAC Certified Forensic Examiner — strong foundational Windows forensics credential and DoD approved. 13Cubed Investigating Windows Endpoints course covers approximately 80% of GCFE content at significant cost savings if employer won't fund SANS training.
OpenText EnCase Examiner — widely deployed in forensics and eDiscovery. Effectively required if your workplace runs EnCase. Requires hands-on EnCase access for the practical exam component.

IACIS CFCE (Certified Forensic Computer Examiner) — the gold standard LE digital forensics credential. Requires law enforcement or investigative professional background and peer review of actual casework. The peer-review process ensures genuine competence rather than just exam performance.

paths.name: Corporate / eDiscovery Track
paths.rationale: For forensic examiners in corporate environments — internal investigations, eDiscovery, HR matters, and IP theft cases without the full LE chain-of-custody burden.
pros: EnCE and ACE widely recognized in corporate forensics and eDiscovery
Commercial tool proficiency directly applicable to corporate workflows
cons: Tool-vendor certs tie recognition to specific commercial platforms
Less recognized in law enforcement contexts than CFCE

prerequisites.experience: IT administration or security operations background beneficial.

cert_sequence.notes: Vendor-neutral forensics foundation with DoD recognition.
EC-Council forensics cert — broad coverage of methodology, tool landscape, and investigation procedures. More accessible than GCFE but less community respected.
OpenText EnCase Examiner — the most recognized commercial forensics tool credential. Widely deployed in corporate forensics and eDiscovery environments.
